problem_summary: 2016 MCM Problem A (continuous): model the temperature of bathtub water in space and time to find a hot-water-injection strategy that keeps the bath uniformly warm and close to the initial comfortable temperature while wasting minimal water, accounting for tub geometry, the bather's body and motions, and the difference made by bubble bath. This O-award paper ("Keep It Warm") builds a layered thermodynamic + optimization solution: a heat-balance ODE for mean temperature, a 3D heat-conduction PDE for the spatial field reduced to a linear plane, a "forced-convection cell" model for human stirring, a Gaussian comfort model, and a multi-objective optimal-control model solved by enumeration, then extends the whole stack to bubble water.
models_used: Universal Thermodynamic Model (UTM): a lumped energy-balance ODE dQ = -dQ_exchange - dQ_evaporation - dQ_body - dQ_radiation + dQ_+water - dQ_-water built from the first law of thermodynamics, tracking internal energy Q = cρV·T̄(t) of the bathtub water

Heat-loss sub-terms each derived from a named physical law: Fourier's law / Newton's law of cooling for wall+air conduction (dQ_exchange), Dalton/Hertz-Knudsen-style surface evaporation rate u(T)=ρ/P·(μ/2πRT)^½·(1-w) for dQ_evaporation, Newton cooling for body absorption dQ_body, and the Stefan-Boltzmann law εσT⁴ for radiation dQ_radiation

3D transient heat-conduction PDE (ρc ∂T/∂t = ∇·(λ∇T) + source) with a Neumann (convective Robin-type) boundary condition -λ∂T/∂n = h(T - T0) on all faces and a point heat source at the tap; solved numerically with the MATLAB PDE Toolbox

Surrogate/approximate analytical solution: linear plane fitting of the PDE numerical surface, T(x,y,z,t) = T̄(t) - K(t)y, with goodness of fit R² = 0.931; gradient K(t) recovered from spatial variance D(T) = a²K²/12

Forced-convection model for human motion: defines a 'forced convection cell' F_k = S_k·V_k (stir distance × displaced volume) and a mixing operator T_{k+1} = T_k - f(F)·(T_k - T̄), with a Gaussian-saturation mixing function f(F) = 1 - exp(-(F/F_b)²) calibrated so f(½V₀a)=95%

Human-comfort model: a normal (Gaussian) distribution of comfort β over temperature, β = 1/(3√2π)·exp(-(T'_B-312)²/9), calibrated to the 306K-318K acceptable band as a ±2σ (95.45%) interval, peak comfort at 312K

Multi-objective optimal-control model of the injection strategy: weighted objective min[λ1·(temperature-deviation integral) + λ2·(water used) - λ3·(comfort β)] subject to flow/temperature bounds, the comfort relation, and the UTM dynamics as constraints; two strategy variants — Strategy I constant trickle (optimize flow k_b, inlet temp T'_b) and Strategy II thermostatic on/off switching with a 0-1 indicator η (optimize T_min1, T_min2, T'_b)

Bubble-bath model: a modified UTM that drops the evaporation term and replaces direct water-air convection with conduction through a 3cm air-bubble insulating layer (λ/d_air), quantifying heat-loss power reduction to 45.85% of normal water
key_techniques: Hierarchical model decomposition: separate time evolution (lumped ODE) from spatial distribution (PDE field), then recombine — the spatial fit T(x,y,z,t)=T̄(t)-K(t)y is fed back into every integral heat term so the ODE stays low-dimensional

Dimensionality reduction by surrogate modeling: replacing an intractable PDE numerical surface with a fitted linear plane (R²=0.931) to obtain a usable closed-form analytic temperature field — explicitly justified by symmetry (drops the spurious x-term) and by 2.5s being the worst-case steepest-gradient moment

Discretizing differential equations: all PDEs/ODEs converted to difference equations (finite-difference / forward Euler) so the optimizer can step the dynamics; optimal parameters found by brute-force enumeration over a grid (k_b step 0.001, T'_b step 0.1)

Physical-constant calibration of model parameters: anchoring f(F) with limiting cases f(0)=0 and f(∞)=1 plus one designed scenario, and fitting the comfort Gaussian to literature scald/cold thresholds (validated against a 320K burn point, 0.31% error)

Quantified, percentage-based result reporting: 2.84% extra water buys 12.66% temperature-stability gain at 91.39% comfort; bubble water cuts heat loss to 45.85% and uses 57.14% of the water — concrete cost/benefit framing

Sign-tracked error analysis: for each neglected effect (humidity, airflow, waves, drop cooling, natural convection, metabolic heat, viscous work) the authors state the direction (above/below zero) of the induced error, building a credible error budget without numbers
Factor-variation (one-at-a-time) sensitivity analysis over ±5% on tub a/b/H, wall h, body area S, and water h_water, reported in tables for both water consumption and water temperature
why_it_won: From a judge's view this paper wins because it converts an everyday, fuzzy prompt into a rigorous, physically-grounded thermodynamic system and then closes the loop to an actionable optimized strategy with hard numbers. It hits every required deliverable (fill/cool/reheat phases, human-motion convection, water-saving strategy, bubble bath, and the one-page layperson note) and answers them with named laws (Fourier, Newton cooling, Stefan-Boltzmann, first law of thermodynamics) rather than hand-waving. The standout original idea — the forced-convection cell F=S_k·V_k with a saturating mixing function — gives a clean, defensible way to quantify the otherwise-intractable 'people stir the water' effect, exactly the kind of creative modeling judges reward. The optimization is genuinely multi-objective (temperature stability vs. water vs. comfort) and yields crisp, interpretable answers (0.007 L/s at 311.1K; bubble water at 0.004 L/s). It is self-aware: the Strategy II result T_min1=T_min2 (instant on/off → continuous injection) is surprising, and the team explains it physically instead of hiding it. Strong validation (comfort model's 320K scald point matches literature to 0.31%, plane fit R²=0.931) and disciplined error/sensitivity sections signal modeling maturity.
section_highlights: The Summary is excellent: it walks the reader through the build order (UTM → OCM → bubble extension → error/sensitivity) and front-loads the punchline numbers (R²=0.931, 0.007 L/s, 311.1K, 45.85%), so a judge knows the whole story in one page. Figure 1 (a model-relationship flowchart from 'initial sub-equations' to 'final model') gives an immediate map of how the five models nest. The derivation in 4.1 is unusually transparent — each of the six heat terms is introduced, justified by a named law, and assigned an equation, then assembled into one master ODE (Eq. 13). The assumptions section (10 numbered items) is a model of the genre: each assumption carries an explicit justification, not just a claim. The separate one-page non-technical 'Explanation' (Section 7, 'Why it's so hard to enjoy a hot bath') directly fulfills the prompt and translates the math into bather advice. The Appendix ships actual MATLAB code for the enumeration solver and the curve generation, reinforcing reproducibility. Weaknesses in presentation: heavy 'Chinglish' phrasing, a few garbled/duplicated equations (e.g., the radiation integral collapses both bracket terms to the same expression; dQ_+water and dQ_-water share a label in Eqs. 8-9), and some results paragraphs in Section 6 have blank placeholders where numbers should be.
pitfalls_or_limitations: The keystone surrogate T(x,y,z,t)=T̄(t)-K(t)y is fit only at one instant (2.5s) from one PDE run and then asserted to hold for all t>2.5s; K(t)'s time evolution is admitted to be never fitted ('with limited time, we don't fit K(t)'), leaving a core function of the model undetermined

Lumped-parameter ODE collapses a real 3D field to a mean temperature, and many transport coefficients (h, h', h_body, λ) are treated as constants pulled from assumptions rather than measured or cited per-material

The signature F_b parameter in the forced-convection mixing function cannot be derived from physical constants and is set by a hand-designed scenario (f(½V₀a)=95%) — the authors themselves flag this as a weakness needing experiment

Optimization is brute-force enumeration on a coarse grid (k_b step 0.001, T'_b step 0.1), so reported optima carry ±half-step error and Strategy II's fine resolution was abandoned for lack of compute — the headline T_min1=T_min2 result is partly an artifact of difference-equation coarseness

Strategies I and II give qualitatively different answers under nominally identical objective weights; the team attributes this to inconsistent normalization of the three objective terms, exposing that the λ-weights are unprincipled and results are sensitive to them

Several equations are typographically broken or mislabeled (radiation integral, the duplicated dQ_+water/dQ_-water tag, an empty β-expression in Strategy II), and Section 6 'Comprehensive Results' has missing numeric fill-ins — sloppy final QA
Many constants/assumptions are unvalidated against any real bathtub experiment; the only external validation is the comfort model vs. one literature scald temperature
reusable_patterns: Split-then-recombine modeling: solve the slow scalar dynamics with a lumped ODE and the spatial structure with a PDE, fit the PDE field to a simple closed form, then substitute that form back into the ODE's integral terms to keep the system solvable — a general recipe for any space-time continuous problem

Surrogate-by-fitting an intractable numerical solution into a low-order analytic expression and reporting R² to justify it; pick the worst-case instant (steepest gradient) to fit so the approximation is conservative elsewhere

Define a custom 'cell'/index to quantify a hard-to-formalize driver (here human stirring as F=distance×displaced-volume) and wrap it in a saturating function f=1-exp(-(F/F_b)²) pinned by limiting cases f(0)=0, f(∞)=1 plus one calibration point

Encode subjective preference as a calibrated Gaussian: set the peak to the ideal value and fix σ by mapping a known acceptable band to a ±2σ (95.45%) interval, then validate the tail against a literature threshold

Multi-objective optimization as a weighted scalarization with user-tunable λ-weights, embedding the governing differential equations directly as constraints; solve by converting to difference equations and enumerating a parameter grid
Use a 0-1 indicator variable η to unify two operating regimes (tap-on injection vs. natural cooling) into a single piecewise differential equation for a switching/thermostatic control problem

Sign-of-error budgeting: for every neglected physical effect, state whether it makes the model over- or under-predict, producing a credible qualitative error analysis when quantitative error is impossible

Always ship the required non-technical one-pager as a self-contained narrative that names the key trade-off (water vs. warmth) and gives concrete advice, plus an appendix with runnable solver code for reproducibility
